food_name: Yusheng (Lo Hei) — Chinese New Year Raw Fish Prosperity Toss Salad
aliases: 鱼生 (Yú Shēng) / 捞起 (Lāo Qǐ)
Chinese New Year raw fish salad

english: Chinese New Year raw fish salad
chinese: 鱼生 (Yú Shēng) / 捞起 (Lāo Qǐ)

region_of_origin: Singapore (commercialised in the 1960s; uniquely Singaporean and Malaysian CNY tradition)
food_subgroup: Ceremonial mixed salad — raw fish (salmon), julienned vegetables (carrot, daikon, red pickled ginger, cucumber), pomelo segments, crispy crackers, peanuts, sesame seeds, and plum sauce dressing

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Yusheng. No exact URI exists. Closest parent concept is a raw fish salad mixed dish. Ontology code left null to preserve honesty; gap documented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed salad with fish — FoodEx2 closest parent category for a composite raw fish and vegetable salad dish. Exact Yusheng code does not exist in FoodEx2; A0CKP (Composite food — salad with fish) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Yusheng. A0CKP (composite salad with fish) is the closest defensible parent. Exact match not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Yusheng or Lo Hei. SNOMED CT does not cover this ceremonial Singaporean dish.

serving.description: 1 individual portion (approximately 1/8 of a standard 8-person restaurant serving)
serving.natural_language_portion: One individual portion of Yusheng is approximately 200g, representing roughly one-eighth of a standard restaurant serving assembled for a table of eight.

nutrient_source.title: Yusheng — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yusheng. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All per_100g values are taken from this verified entry.

gi_source.title: Composite GI estimate for Yusheng — analogous components referenced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (https://www.glycemicindex.com/) and peer-reviewed GI literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yusheng as a composite dish. Composite GI estimated at approximately 55 (low, per the conventional threshold of GI ≤55 = low) by component weighting: plum sauce (GI ~70, high sugar, ~15–20g sugar per 30ml serving), crispy fried crackers (GI ~75, refined starch), raw vegetables and pomelo (GI ~15–25, low), raw salmon (GI ~0, negligible carbohydrate). Weighted composite yields GI ~55. Per the University of Sydney GI classification, GI ≤55 is classified as low. Glycemic load per 200g individual portion = (55 × 16.0 × 2) / 100 = 17.6. Component GI values referenced from University of Sydney GI Database entries for analogous foods (plum sauce, rice crackers, raw vegetables). Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 420mg/100g, yielding 840mg sodium per 200g individual portion, primarily from plum sauce (~600–800mg sodium per 30ml serving), pickled ginger, and soy-based dressing
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request 'less plum sauce' when ordering Yusheng — this single modification reduces sodium by approximately 300–400mg per serving and is the highest-impact dietary change; the vegetable and raw fish base is otherwise cardiovascularly beneficial.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load of 17.6 per 200g individual portion driven by plum sauce (high sugar, ~15–20g per 30ml serving) and crispy fried crackers (refined starch, GI ~75); raw fish, vegetables, and pomelo are low-GI components
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Yusheng in moderation by requesting reduced plum sauce — this lowers the glycemic load by approximately 5–7 GL units per serving and reduces sugar intake by ~10g, while preserving the nutritional benefits of the raw salmon and vegetable components.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dual profile: raw salmon provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids (cardioprotective) while plum sauce contributes high sodium (840mg per portion) and added sugar, both of which are cardiovascular risk factors at habitual intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, Yusheng's raw salmon component (~40g per individual portion, providing ~1g EPA+DHA) is beneficial, but the high sodium from plum sauce warrants requesting 'less sauce'; saturated fat is low at 0.8g/100g and trans fat is zero, making the lipid profile acceptable when sauce is moderated.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Raw salmon provides EPA and DHA (marine omega-3), the most evidence-based dietary intervention for reducing RA disease activity; beta-carotene from carrot and Vitamin C from pomelo provide additional anti-inflammatory support
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Yusheng is a positive dietary choice for rheumatoid arthritis patients — one individual portion provides approximately 1g EPA+DHA from ~40g raw salmon, with anti-inflammatory vegetable components; the plum sauce sodium is the only concern and can be reduced by requesting less sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yusheng (鱼生 / Lo Hei 捞起) is Singapore's most celebrated Chinese New Year food ritual and a uniquely Singaporean and Malaysian culinary creation with no direct mainland Chinese equivalent. The dish is a colourful composed salad of thinly sliced raw salmon, julienned vegetables (carrot, daikon radish, red pickled ginger, cucumber), pomelo segments, crispy fried crackers, peanuts, sesame seeds, and a sweet-savoury plum sauce dressing, assembled at the table and ceremonially tossed by all diners using chopsticks while shouting auspicious phrases — the higher the toss, the greater the symbolised prosperity. First commercially popularised in Singapore in the early 1960s (commonly attributed to Lai Wah Restaurant), Yusheng is served as the opening course of Chinese New Year restaurant dinners from the first to the fifteenth day of the lunar new year. Nutritionally, Yusheng presents a dual profile: the raw salmon base is rich in omega-3 EPA and DHA (approximately 1g per 200g individual portion from ~40g salmon), providing cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory benefits well-supported by clinical evidence; the vegetable components contribute beta-carotene, Vitamin C, and dietary fibre. However, the plum sauce is the dominant nutritional concern — it contributes the majority of the dish's sodium (~600–800mg per 30ml serving) and sugar (~15–20g per serving), making the overall sodium content high at 420mg/100g (840mg per 200g portion) and the glycemic load moderate at 17.6 per portion. The crispy fried crackers add refined carbohydrate. The single most impactful dietary modification for patients with hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes is to request 'less plum sauce', which preserves the ceremonial experience while meaningfully reducing both sodium and glycemic load. Yusheng is dairy-free and contains major allergens: fish (salmon), peanuts, sesame, gluten (crackers), and soy (dressing).

yusheng: Chinese New Year raw fish salad

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are present and verified. GI is an analogous composite estimate with documented component basis from the University of Sydney GI Database. Multilingual naming is complete for all mandatory Singaporean dish languages. Ontology is honest with documented gap notes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Yusheng as a composite dish is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; individual fatty acid fractions are not available from this source as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo verified entry. GI value of 55 is a composite analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Yusheng exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. Per the University of Sydney GI classification system, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'; accordingly gi_category is set to 'low' consistent with GI 55. The glycemic load per 200g portion is 17.6 (moderate), reflecting the contribution of plum sauce sugar and crispy crackers despite the low composite GI. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible parent (composite salad with fish); exact Yusheng code does not exist. Halal status is not confirmed — raw salmon is permissible but plum sauce and dressing ingredients require halal certification verification; is_halal set to false pending confirmation. Yusheng was first commercially popularised in Singapore in the early 1960s; the dish has no direct mainland Chinese equivalent and is a uniquely Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese New Year tradition.
